issue_title: certbot-auto stores configuration in /~/ directory when launched as systemd service
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):

Debian 8 amd64

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
```
# wget -q -O certbot-auto https://dl.eff.org/certbot-auto
# chmod a+x certbot-auto 
# mv certbot-auto /usr/local/bin/certbot
```

## I ran this command and it produced this output:

I create initial certificates like this:
```
certbot certonly --non-interactive --agree-tos --no-self-upgrade --email info@domain.com --apache -d my.domain.com
```
Certbot creates `/root/.local/share/letsencryt`file tree:
```
# tree -a /root/.local/ | head
/root/.local/
└── share
    └── letsencrypt
        ├── bin
        │   ├── activate
        │   ├── activate.csh
        │   ├── activate.fish
        │   ├── activate_this.py
        │   ├── certbot
        │   ├── easy_install
```

BUT, 

After declaring systemd unit file `/etc/systemd/system/certbot.service` (to be usde by `certbot.timer`) like this:
```
[Unit]
Description=Certbot

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/certbot --no-self-upgrade -q renew
PrivateTmp=true
```
And execute `systemctl start certbot.service` (or by setting up `certbot.timer`),  Certbot creates different configuration file tree in system root, in `~` directory:
```
# tree -a /~/ | head                                                                                                                                     
/~/
└── .local
    └── share
        └── letsencrypt
            ├── bin
            │   ├── activate
            │   ├── activate.csh
            │   ├── activate.fish
            │   ├── activate_this.py
            │   ├── certbot

```

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:

Certbot duplicates `.local/share/letsencrypt` in different path when executed as systemd service, compared to execution interactively in terminal. Seems like "~/" is not resolved when running as systemd service.

## Here is a Certbot log showing the issue (if available):
There is no relevant log entries.

issue_body:
I am using `certbot renew -q` from within a cronjob. I noticed the debug log only contains the debug stuff, but not the normal output that is usually written to stdout (when not using `-q`).
However, the debug information doesn't help in analyzing what went wrong without the normal output giving some context ("processing ...conf", "no renewals attempted", etc).

I'd thus suggest to always write the latter details to the logfile, too (most importantly when `-q` is used, but I think it's helpful in any case)

0.8.1 on debian

issue_title: query_param decorator does not preserve signature
issue_body:
The `query_param` decorator is used on pretty much all client methods. It uses `functools.wraps` to copy `__module__`, `__name__`, `__doc__` etc. Unfortunately, `wraps` does not preserve the signature of the wrapped function, so `inspect.getargspec()` and friends become pretty much useless (`inspect.signature()` works but only in Python 3.5+, and only because it cheats).

This makes it difficult for e.g. code completion in IDEs to work correctly. On a more egoistic note, it also makes it more difficult for things like Elastic APM to introspect the client 😊 

Preserving the signature is a bit less trivial than preserving `__doc__` etc, but there are a few utilities that could help: 

 * [`decorator.py`](https://github.com/micheles/decorator)
 * [boltons](https://github.com/mahmoud/boltons) (specifically `boltons.funcutils.wraps`)

There's also [wrapt](https://github.com/GrahamDumpleton/wrapt), but that might be a bit too heavy just to preserve the signature.

We could either add one of the two libraries as a dependency, or vendor them (they are both BSD).

issue_title: NuPIC Installs and Runs on Windows OS
issue_body:
It would be great to have NuPIC install and run out-of-the-box on Windows operating system. This would extend our user-base considerably. 

There is a **ton** of work going on in the https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/pull/184 PR by @rcrowder and @kandeel. While that work is heading in the right direction and it's awesome to see such wonderful progress (and see an AppConveyor build running and passing!!), the PR is so large it's pretty much un-mergeable. I don't think we can allow all the third party code into our codebase. At the very least, someone will need to research the licenses for all this code, and it would be hard to believe that they will all be compatible with GPLv3. 

I think the right way forward is to figure out how to install these dependencies without polluting the `nupic.core` codebase with a bunch of third-party code. 

Depends on https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/issues/266.

* * *

- [ ] decide whether we are going to use NuGet or pip for installation on Windows
- [ ] [Set up Numenta NuGet account](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/issues/1556)

issue_title: Special token index guarantee.
issue_body:
**Context**
Special tokens are frequently used for masking or padding or interpreting the model. It's important in a Encoder/Decoder context that the decoder and encoder share the same indexes for EOS, SOS, and PAD. 

**Problem**
Creating two fields, one for French and one for English, how does one know the index of EOS without per class `self.stoi[eos_token]`?
How does the code guarantee that the French dictionary has the same EOS index as the English dictionary?

**Solution**
With setting the optional parameter 'eos_token' would it be possible to set 'eos_token_index'?

issue_title: Proposal: Add "filters"
issue_body:
Currently SatPy has the idea of readers, writers, compositors, and modifiers. I would like to add a "filter" object type. @mraspaud and I have been talking about this on and off for a while so I thought I'd make the discussion a little more official.

A Filter is an optional object or function that when enabled checks the currently loaded datasets against some condition. If the condition is satisfied then the Dataset does not continue on in processing. Otherwise the Dataset continues on as normal. A Filter should *not* modify the values inside a Dataset (only modifiers should do this). However, a Filter may filter out individual pixels if it makes sense.

During our last discussion is was decided that Filters are executed before composites are generated and after composites are generated. I'm not sure whether or not the "before" execution is done inside the readers.

Use cases (please add more as they come up):

1. Day or night filter: useful for reflectances that shouldn't be generated if a scene is more than X% night time. Or 'fog' like composites that are only useful if it is X% night time. How does this differ from per-pixel filtering?
2. Time of year filter: Some products or composites are most useful at a certain time of year. I've seen one case like this with Polar2Grid where there is a certain stretching/enhancement applied to a product that makes it "summer land surface temperature", but another stretching is just "land surface temperature". Not sure if there are others.

issue_title: Wickman's proposal for pants reorg
issue_body:
When pants moves to its own repository, I'd like to take the opportunity to do a code reorg in order to make it easier for other projects / repositories / organizations to use pants.
### current layout
Backends (java, scala, python, etc) consist of tasks and targets, and basic wiring into phases. Right now the code org is something like this:
* **pants.bin**
* **pants.base**
* pants.commands
* pants.doc
* **pants.goal**
* pants.java
* pants.python
* pants.pants_doc
* pants.python
* pants.targets
* pants.tasks

Bolded is what I consider to be "pants core" – as in pants simply can't operate without code in there. Specifically this is the execution engine and ancillary stuff like base classes for everything else (Task, Goal, Target.) Currently the Task/TaskError base classes are in pants.tasks.__init__ but should be moved into pants.base.

While pants/__init__.py contains some code necessary for pants to run (get_buildroot), most of it should be moved out to the leaves (is_* e.g. is_java, is_scala) and 'from twitter.pants.targets import *' should go away entirely.
### new backend layout
I propose that everything in pants.java, pants.python, pants.targets and pants.tasks be reorganized (temporarily) into a new toplevel directory:
* pants.backend

With the following layout:
* pants.backend.{language}.__init__
* pants.backend.{language}.base
* pants.backend.{language}.targets
* pants.backend.{language}.tasks

Each backend is treated as a namespace package so that they can more easily be developed by third parties. This model works naturally with the python.new backend, and for the most part with the Java and Scala backends.

One case where this is more challenging is IDL backends. My proposal for handling this is by making the CodeGen(Task) base class in pants.base the canonical base class for code generation, and specific pants.backend.thrift and pants.backend.protobuf IDL backends that other backends can then depend upon. For example, pants.backend.python would depend upon pants.backend.thrift, and subclass the ThriftGen base from pants.backend.thrift.base to create a Python-specific codegen target. (I've already done the refactor in the python.new branch to make this more straightforward through language-specific createtarget abstract base classes.) So rather than the 'gen' phase with 'gen:thrift' and 'gen:protobuf' goals, you'd have the 'gen' phase with 'gen:thrift-python', 'gen:thrift-java', 'gen:protobuf-go' etc.
### bootstrapping
To bootstrap a repository to use pants, you would do the following:
1. copy the bootstrap_pants.py script into your repository and name it 'pants', chmod +x
2. add a root BUILD file which sets up your source root and defines which plugins you'd like

The pants bootstrap script currently bootstraps itself from PyPI, though some of the dependent source dists have not yet been published (specifically twitter.pants, twitter.common-core, twitter.common.python) but we can do that when we're ready – especially since twitter.pants could be 'pb' or 'pantsbuild' or something else. If you don't have external network access, you'd add a field in pants.ini that specifies where the bootstrapper should be looking for artifacts and you'd cache them locally. Similarly, by default the bootstrap script bootstraps the latest version of pants, but you could address specific versions in the pants.ini using the existing Requirement format (e.g. 'pb==0.3.2' or 'pb>0.3,<1').

The bootstrapper script would write pants.pex and on subsequent runs 1) check the pants.pex version to make sure it's compatible with the one defined in pants.ini and if so 2) os.execv.
### repository BUILD
The root BUILD would look like:
```python
require('pants.backend.java==0.2.3')
require('pants.backend.python')
require_path('plugins/foursquare')
# [any extra project specific methods or variables defined here]
```
Each backend would be a PythonLibrary with a corresponding SetupPy artifact, which can have install_requires in order to dictate dependencies (e.g. pystache, boto, pants.backend.thrift, whatever.) The upside being that 1) this is all already implemented and 2) they could write a backend as a standard setup.py project outside of pants.

require_path would simply look for an __init__.py in that directory and install everything there into the root namespace.

Currently for BUILD files to be usable, we autoinject 'from pants.targets import *' and 'from twitter.common.quantity import Amount, Time' in every single one. Instead, we'd deposit the union of the namespaces created by the buildroot BUILD, and the __init__.py of each registered backend into all down-tree BUILDs. The backend _init_s would also be responsible for wiring tasks into phases.

issue_title: API refactor for rlp.Serializable
issue_body:
I've some thoughts on how we might change `rlp.Serializable`.

### Current features

- immutable vs. mutable objects (`make_mutable`, `make_immutable`).
- attribute access to fields
- instantiation with either `*args` or `**kwargs`
- `cls.exclude` API for creating classes with a subset of fields


### Inefficiencies

- mutability checks at runtime
- field assignment done exclusively through `__setattr__` (60% faster to use direct attribute assignment)
- `obj.fields` is non-ideal since it disallows the use of any field named `fields`


### Solution

- use `collections.namedtuple` for immutable versions of the object.  Fields are naturally ordered, and immutability is enforced by default.
- use a dynamically constructed class for mutable versions of the object.

issue_title: getCTS() or cts always returns false in Linux, but works in Windows
issue_body:
Test on Windows:
```
>>> import serial
>>> p = serial.Serial(port="COM14", baudrate=9600)
>>> p.cts # after pulling CTS pin high on FT232
False
>>> p.cts  # after pulling CTS pin low on FT232
True
```

Test on Linux or on Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) (result is same)
```
>>> import serial
>>> p = serial.Serial(port="/dev/ttyS14", baudrate=9600)
>>> p.cts # after pulling CTS pin high on FT232
False
>>> p.cts  # after pulling CTS pin low on FT232
False    <-- Note This should be True
```
What's extra-confusing is that when I saw that it failed on a real Linux machine, I figured that maybe FTDI had a bug in their Linux driver, so I tried it on WSL (which I believe uses the Windows driver on the host machine and provides what the Linux subsystem perceives as a plain old serial port, thus not using the Linux FTDI driver at all).  The result was the same as the bare-metal Linux system -- cts is always false, regardless of true state of hardware line.

--Jim

issue_title: After 1.6.0 identity_shortener fails with 'UUID' object has no attribute '__getitem__'
django-libsass should apply CssAbsoluteFilter itself, in case it's disabled at the django-compressor level
issue_body:
Related: https://github.com/django-compressor/django-compressor/issues/226

When precompilers (such as django-libsass) are in use but compression is disabled, files are served from `/static/CACHE/` but django-compressor's CssAbsoluteFilter does not run. As a result, relative paths within the CSS are treated differently depending on whether or not compression is active.

In the absence of a solution to this problem at the django-compressor level, we can work around this by running CssAbsoluteFilter as part of the precompilation step, as described here: https://github.com/django-compressor/django-compressor/issues/226#issuecomment-57859011. I propose to incorporate this workaround into django-libsass itself.

It doesn't matter if django-compressor subsequently runs CssAbsoluteFilter again, because CssAbsoluteFilter leaves existing absolute paths intact - however, it will add a cache-busting suffix to them, so ideally we should run a monkey-patched version CssAbsoluteFilter that skips this step, to avoid this happening twice.

issue_title: session_security breaks "End Session" on the current session when using user_sessions
issue_body:
The middleware

user_sessions.middleware.SessionMiddleware

Provides a listing of a user's sessions across various clients.  The user can then choose to "End Session" on any of their existing sessions, including their current session they are logged in as.

When using the user_sessions middleware alone, clicking "End Session" will behave the same way as "Logout".  Unfortunately, when combined with the session_security middleware, clicking "End Session" on the current session has no effect.

With some pdb tracing, I've figured out the following rough series of events:

1) request:session_security updates the last_activity in the session
2) request:user_sessions deletes the session object
3) response:user_sessions detects the session was modified in step 1) and re-saves the session to the backing DB

The user is redirected to the same Session List page, with their current session still active.

issue_title: Don't reset `getLastVersionForProfile` when installing orphaned upgrade steps
issue_body: Currently when installing only an orphaned upgrade step, the last profile version is reset to that step's version. This results in the the upgrades that have a bigger timestamp than the orphaned step being proposed for installation again after the orphaned step was installed.

class TestJsonpickle(unittest.TestCase):

        obj = TestClass()

issue_title: Bundle resource jars do not include resource parent dirs.
issue_body:
For example, applying:
```diff
diff --git a/testprojects/src/java/org/pantsbuild/testproject/ideacodeandresources/BUILD b/testprojects/src/java/org/pantsbuild/testproject/ideacodeandresources/BUILD
index 1c7aba8..0f33a6f 100644
--- a/testprojects/src/java/org/pantsbuild/testproject/ideacodeandresources/BUILD
+++ b/testprojects/src/java/org/pantsbuild/testproject/ideacodeandresources/BUILD
@@ -16,3 +16,10 @@ resources(
   sources=['README.md'],
 )
 
+jvm_binary(
+  name='bin',
+  main='None!',
+  dependencies=[
+    ':code',
+  ],
+)
```

Then running `./pants clean-all bundle testprojects/src/java/org/pantsbuild/testproject/ideacodeandresources:bin`, yields:
```
$ zipinfo -1 dist/testprojects.src.java.org.pantsbuild.testproject.ideacodeandresources.bin-bundle/libs/testprojects.src.java.org.pantsbuild.testproject.ideacodeandresources.readme-0.jar 
org/pantsbuild/testproject/ideacodeandresources/README.md
```

Java jar files generally need all parent dir entries for any given entry to enable expected use cases and we enforce this with [tests](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/tests/java/org/pantsbuild/tools/jar/JarBuilderTest.java#L411-421) for our java [JarBuilder](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/java/org/pantsbuild/tools/jar/JarBuilder.java) code.

issue_title: Add support for IE to CSP middleware
issue_body:

the CSP header should be `X-Content-Security-Policy` instead of `Content-Security-Policy`.

Both headers can not be set at the same time, as it will cause unexpected behaviours on certain versions of browsers.

In my particular use case
* if the user has not made any changes and the cloud is synchronized then it would show `cloud-synchronized`
* if the user has made changes it shows the `cloud-upload` icon, the user can then push the changes to the cloud and once those changes have synchronized successfully then it shows `cloud-synchronized` until other changes are made by the user

issue_title: Yaml Feed not in valid format?
issue_body:
Hey,

I've seen requests for a json feed to allow us to do our own searches for icons, but I see you have yaml feed, so I'm using that instead. The only small problem I have with it is the feed doesn't appear to be 100% correct in some places:
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/FortAwesome/Font-Awesome/master/src/icons.yml

If you search for ban (f05e), envelope (f0e0) or windows (f17a), you can see that although they appear to have filters, they don't have a filter heading in the yaml. The effect of this (in my implementation at least) is the created field gets deserialized as "1.0 - delete - remove - trash - hide - block - stop", so rather than storing it as a double as I'd prefer, I'm forced into using a string. This would obviously limit my ability to do my own filters against the icon set for these three instances.

Additionally, I think this may be having the same limitation on the main bootstrap search (assuming it uses the same yml file). For example, if I search for one of the filters assigned to check-circle-o, the icon appears in the search, but if I search for "delete", which is a filter for ban, the ban icon does not appear. The same behaviour is seen for the other two icons that appear to be missing the filter title in the yml.

issue_title: Icon-request: fa-card
issue_body:
I'm an artist, and my web site allows viewing my work as thumbnails or in a "news feed" view (similar to Instagram or Facebook). I'm using `fa-th` for thumbnails, but there's no good icon for the "feed" view. `fa-bars`, `fa-photo`, and `th-list` aren't a good match. The one I ended up using is `fa-square`, but it's pretty generic.

So I'd like to propose a new icon, `fa-card`, that somewhat resembles a Polaroid photograph -- a tallish rectangle outline, with a filled rectangle inside, vertically aligned close to the top.

![example-fa-card](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2041718/11916252/15d189e0-a694-11e5-9317-30c695105a0a.png)

Both Semantic UI and Google's Material Design refer to this UI convention as a "card," and that seems like a good name since `fa-feed` is already taken for RSS/Atom feeds.

https://www.google.com/design/spec/components/cards.html
http://semantic-ui.com/views/card.html

I suspect this could be useful for many web applications where data are represented as a box with a photograph at the top and text below.

To see my web site in action, go to one of the galleries (http://www.tallent.us/) and look in the "Galleries" drop-down menu. *Please note that my site is NSFW* (nude photography).

issue_title: renew() stops for any unhandled exception
issue_body: If we get an unhandled exception during the renewal attempt, it doesn't crash the client but it does make `renew()` stop at that point. For example, try setting `standalone_supported_challenges` to an invalid value in a lineage's renewal configuration file, then running `renew()`. When it tries to renew that lineage, it will crash with an exception, and no subsequent lineages will be renewed. This is presumably the case with respect to every kind of unhandled exception that might occur for any reason.

issue_title: A few features from "colloids tooltik"
issue_body:
Hi,

First, let me express my awe relative to the work you have done with Trackpy. This is really a great package, well integrated in the modern scientific Python ecosystem.

A while ago, during my own PhD thesis, I wrote some code, first in C++, then in Python to track particles from confocal images. You can find this code [here](https://github.com/MathieuLeocmach/colloids), a paper that describes part of it [here](http://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlelanding/2013/sm/c2sm27107a) or on [ArXiv](http://arxiv.org/abs/1301.7237). It is stable, fast, running on a daily basis, but minimally documented. Since I have little time to maintain my own package and since Trackpy contains 90% of its features (plus others) I think it would be useful for everybody that I port the 10% missing features into Trackpy. What do you think about it?

**First**, a very simple feature: isotropic bandpassing. Trackpy follows Crocker&Grier algorithm in bandpassing the image by a Gaussian filter (low pass) and an uniform filter (high pass). However the later is not isotropic: it has a square shape. This leads to anisotropic shifts in the particle positions. Typically particles aligned along the diagonal of the image will be localised closer together than particles aligned along the vertical or horizontal direction. Do you have a test to check that?

A quick fix would be to edit the `preprocessing.bandpass` function so that the high pass filter is changed into a Gaussian. In theory the computation time can be higher, especially if the dimension of the kernel is large, but separability of the Gaussian filter and better cache access should make this change painless.

**Second**, a feature that need to be compared with your implementation: subpixel refinement using Newton tangent method. C&G uses centre-of-mass approach. I am aware that you have refined that by iterating. However this approach is biased by the unknown level of the background (see _Feng, Y., Goree, J., & Liu, B. (2007). [Accurate particle position measurement from images.](http://doi.org/10.1063/1.2735920) The Review of Scientific Instruments, 78(5), 53704._ especially their equation 2). A particle only slightly brighter than its background will be shifted more than a well contrasted particle.

A way to get rid of this effect of the background is to use [Newton's method](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_method). In short, if you know a point $x_0$ close to where a function $f$ goes through 0, you can refine this approximation into 

$$x_1 = x_0 - f(x_0)/f'(x_0)$$ 

Here we are looking for a maximum of $f$, where its derivative $f'$ goes through 0, so we compute 

$$x_1 = x_0 - f'(x_0)/f"(x_0)$$

Since we are on a lattice, the first and second derivatives have to be computed by [finite difference](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Finite_difference). Beware! One must use a kernel of size 5 for the second derivative, but it may seems that a kernel of size 3 for the first derivative is enough. This is not so, both must computed with a kernel of the same size (5). Long story short, the coefficients are respectively

```python
coefprime = np.array([1,-8, 0, 8, -1])
coefsec = np.array([-1, 16, -30, 16, -1])

centers[p,1] = i - (im[i-2:i+3,j] * coefprime).sum() / (imim[i-2:i+3,j] * coefsec)
```

This could be implemented in `feature._refine`.

**Third**, a new feature: deconvolution of 3D confocal images. This is detailed in my paper above as well on a [blog post](http://lamson-research.blogspot.jp/2015/03/multiscale-localisation-in-3d-and.html#Simple-deconvolution-of-a-confocal-image).  My implementation is in [this file](https://github.com/MathieuLeocmach/colloids/blob/master/python/colloids/track.py) in the functions `get_deconv_kernel` and `deconvolve`.

This could be implemented as an optional preprocessing in `preprocessing.py`.

**Fourth**, in the long run: multiscale tracking. C&G algorithm is efficient to localise fairly monodisperse particles. For polydisperse assemblies, a multiscale approach is needed. This is the main point of the [paper](http://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlelanding/2013/sm/c2sm27107a) and of [this blog post](http://lamson-research.blogspot.jp/2015/02/multiscale-localisation-in-2d-howto.html). This is fairly long to implement.

Again, what do you think of all of this?

issue_title: Feedback and questions
issue_body:
hi Guys,

I do like the product. 

The following is the feedback and questions:

**/password/reset**

If you provide invalid email it does return you 200 HTTP code and doesn't tell you that email is not found in the database. I'd like to get a different response back, such as 404 with "details": "such email doesn't exists"

**How to integrate with custom model inherited from django.contrib.auth.user model?**

Would be awesome if small tutorial is provided

**Update profile - 3 methods?**

What's the motivation to make a 3 different methods to update profile /me and POST /username, POST /password? I think it's nicer to just call PUT /me method to update username, password. Otherwise a client should hit 3 different API endpoints. 

**How to update other fields of profile with PUT /me?**

It seems right now it only updates email field and nothing else. What about first_name, last_name? What about other custom fields of the model (question #2 above)?

**HTTP Error codes (404, 422)**

For example when you do POST /login with non-existing username you get this

```
400
{
  "non_field_errors": [
    "Unable to login with provided credentials."
  ]
}
```
but it's correct request, while the response says: 400 (Bad Request). It would be nicer to have 404, error.

Also when you do  POST /register and provide non-unique username you get this:

```
400
{
  "username": [
    "This field must be unique."
  ]
}
```
however this is a business logic error and code 422 is better for such use case

**Generic message**

If I do POST /username and provide incorrect value of current_password I get this:

```
400
{
  "current_password": [
    "Invalid password."
  ]
}
```

Which makes it hard for the client (say Android mobile app) as I need to know and define maps ahead of time.

Would not it better to return something like this:

```
422
{
  "errors": 
      [
           {"field": "password", "message""Invalid password provided"},
           ....
     ]
}
```
the same would be for registration and other methods. That way I don't need to define the model for each JSON response body, as they're all generic. Otherwise for each API call you have to create a custom parser (and you have to know all the fields ahead of time). 

**Validation for Incorrect request**

If I do:
*PUT /me*
```
{
    "email": "admin@admin.com",
    "eeee": "jim",
    "zzz": "smith"
}
```

I'll get 200 HTTP code back:

```
{
  "email": "admin@admin.com",
  "id": 1,
  "username": "admin"
}
```
However the example above submitted 2 random fields. I would value if API return something like this:

```
422 or 400
{
  "errors": 
      [
           {"field": "eee", "message""Unknown field"},
           {"field": "zzz", "message""Unknown field"},
           ....
     ]
}
```

issue_title: offsets_for_times support?
issue_body:
I've written a Kafka consumer using the CPP version of librdkafka, and it works great! However, I am currently trying to recreate this consumer in a more light-weight Python version but I am running into some issues with regards to starting from a specific offset.

In my CPP code I used the following calls to set the partition offsets to a before a specific timestamp:
```
partition->set_offset(ts);
...
consumer->offsetsForTimes(partitions, 5000);
```

The `offsetsForTimes` call uses the `rd_kafka_offsets_for_times` function to retrieve specific offset values for a given timestamp, but I cannot find the corresponding call in the Python version of the Kafka consumer. I could not find a similar function in confluent_kafka.c so I was wondering if this feature is support in the Python client, and if so, how to use it.

issue_title: Anonymous order verification hash uses insecure md5
issue_body:
In `src/oscar/apps/order/abstract_models.py`, the `AbstractOrder` model has a `verification_hash` method, which uses the insecure MD5 hashing algorithm to compute a verification code.

I Am Not A Cryptographer, but my spidey sense is tingling...

- `key = '%s%s' % (self.number, settings.SECRET_KEY)`  <- user already knows the number; hash input is a simple concatenation of number and secret key
- `hash = hashlib.md5(key.encode('utf8'))` <- hash is also known to the user

Wouldn't that make the site-wide secret key recoverable from an anonymous order URL?

issue_title: Unnecessary dependency on `pathlib` when running in python3
issue_body:
### Expected behaviour
Datacube shouldn't depend on unnecessary packages when running in Python 3.

### Actual behaviour
There's a dependency on `pathlib`, which is included in the Python 3 standard library, and so doesn't need to be installed.

This causes trouble on the NCI deployment when trying to load `stats` modules which use the `setuptools` entry_points for their registration. And returns error messages to users trying to load them.

### Steps to reproduce the behaviour
```
module load agdc-py3-prod agdc_statistics
dra547@raijin4:~ $ python
Python 3.6.3 | packaged by conda-forge | (default, Nov  4 2017, 10:10:56)
[GCC 4.8.2 20140120 (Red Hat 4.8.2-15)] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import datacube_stats.statistics
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/g/data/v10/public/modules/agdc_statistics/0.9a7/lib/python3.6/site-packages/datacube_stats/statistics.py", line 769, in <module>
    STATS[entry_point.name] = entry_point.load()
  File "/g/data/v10/public/modules/agdc-py3-env/20171214/envs/agdc/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 2404, in load
    self.require(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/g/data/v10/public/modules/agdc-py3-env/20171214/envs/agdc/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 2427, in require
    items = working_set.resolve(reqs, env, installer, extras=self.extras)
  File "/g/data/v10/public/modules/agdc-py3-env/20171214/envs/agdc/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 870, in resolve
    raise DistributionNotFound(req, requirers)
pkg_resources.DistributionNotFound: The 'pathlib' distribution was not found and is required by datacube
>>>
```

### The Fix
Modify `setup.py` to use [platform specific dependencies](https://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/setuptools.html#declaring-platform-specific-dependencies) to only require `pathlib` when not running on python 3.

issue_title: python-escpos prevents Flask web server from logging
issue_body:
<!--
Please feel free to delete any sections that aren't relevant.
-->

<!-- mark with x between the [ ] -->
I have:
- [x ] searched open and closed issues for duplicates

### Bug description
Flask and python-escpos seems not to be working well together. I have created a simple script that creates a flask web server and imports the `Network` class from `escpos.printer`. The web server seems to be working fine but I get no logging whatsoever on the console like Flask usually does.

Here's the script
```
from flask import Flask
from escpos.printer import Network

app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route("/")
def index():
    return "hello world"

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run()
```

I've found that when commenting out `from escpos.printer import Network` Flask runs perfectly fine, and logs to the console like this

```
    * Running on http://127.0.0.1:5000/ (Press CTRL+C to quit)
```

But when it's not, the web server seems to be running still, but the problem is that it can't log to the console which is a feature I would need.

I've digged into Flask source code and found out that it uses `werkzeug.serving._log()` to do its logging but I don't see how this would be affected by python-escpos. Also, normal prints work.

### Steps to reproduce
- Create script like above
- Run with `python p.py`

<!-- since version 2.0.1 you can type 'python-escpos version' in your shell.
Alternatively you could use '__version__' in module escpos. -->
 **python-escpos version:** 3.0a3

 **python version:** 3.6.4

 **operating system:** Windows 10

In the common case (e.g., python APIs), we should allow the users to prepare their data using RDD, and BigDL can then automatically transform these into batches for training:

2) Optimizer & Validator can automatically transform that into Sample and MiniBatch for training

issue_title: Use of query in with_request isn't documented
issue_body:
.with_request has the parameter 'query' to pass parameters on the url:

`/users/?limit=25` becomes `.with_request('get', '/users/', query={'limit': 25})`

This example usage appears in https://github.com/pact-foundation/pact-python/blob/master/e2e/contracts/test_e2e.py but isn't in the main README.md.

I suggest documenting this parameter, assuming it is safe to use.

issue_title: Boilerplate functionality for processor modules
issue_body:
# Overview

`ingest` and `spew` are awesome little functions, but I found there was still a lot of boilerplate to be done for each processor. I suggest writing some additional wrapper code for dealing with that.

## Assumptions

I assume that most of the time, you either want to process row by rows and/or mutate the datapackage.

## Ojbectives

The "wrapper" code would fulfill 2 purposes:

    1. Provide boilerplate functionality
        - Log the processor parameters
        - Force the iteration over a given sample size
        - Log a sample output of the processor
        - Handle a chosen set of exceptions

    2. Pass context to the row processor
        - Passes row and resource indices to the processor
        - Manages parameters defaults
        - Passes parameters to the row processor
        - Add row and resource in

issue_title: user PKs should be strings in API responses
issue_body:
I believe that (for consistency) we should treat all PKs as strings, even if they happen to be implemented as integers on the server.

For API endpoints such as `facilityuser` and the various coach reports, this means that user pks will be set as e.g. `"2"` instead of `2`

issue_title: TaskQueue behavior for duplicate tasks in a list during Queue.add()
issue_body:
I have some questions about the behavior of `taskqueue.Queue().add(task_list)` when 1 of the tasks is duplicate, but the others are not.

I am not aware of any documentation around the taskqueue API that explains this behavior - as such it seems undefined.

My concern is that adding a list of multiple tasks, where 1 may already have been tombstoned/created, may cause the other legitimate tasks to fail.

This affects the pipeline library in the following codeblock:

```python
try:
  taskqueue.Queue(self.queue_name).add(task_list)
except (taskqueue.TombstonedTaskError, taskqueue.TaskAlreadyExistsError):
  pass
```
> https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-pipelines/blob/bd9fa1e7000dc6af6fe704137cc53fa6d8cde427/python/src/pipeline/pipeline.py#L1659-L1663

Any clues? This may be a good question for @tkaitchuck.
This was brought up in issue #54.

issue_title: Starting a task on a local module without an explicit version fails
issue_body:
The root cause of this is [this GAE dev_server issue](https://code.google.com/p/googleappengine/issues/detail?id=13178&thanks=13178&ts=1470144087): when running a module locally with `dev_appserver.py` which doesn't have `version:` in `app.yaml`, it will have something like `"None.175924092260391774` in `os.environ['CURRENT_VERSION_ID']`. This results in `util._get_task_target()` returning something like `"None.modulename"` which then raises a `InvalidModuleError` when trying to enqueue the task in [`_PipelineContext.start`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-pipelines/blob/277394648dac3e8214677af898935d07399ac8e1/python/src/pipeline/pipeline.py#L1839).

One possible solution would be:

- hopefully the [GAE dev_server bug](https://code.google.com/p/googleappengine/issues/detail?id=13178&thanks=13178&ts=1470144087) gets fixed quickly
- to migrate `util._get_task_target()` over to modules (since it's using undocumented APIs). Something like:

````Python
from google.appengine.api import modules

# ...

def _get_task_target():
  # ...
  module = modules.get_current_module_name()
  version = modules.get_current_version_name()
  # workaround until https://code.google.com/p/googleappengine/issues/detail?id=13178& gets fixed
  if version == "None": version = None 

  if module == "default":
    return version
  if version is None:
    return module
  return "%s.%s" % (version, module)
````

issue_title: Usage of leading underscores
issue_body:
Normal Python usage, of course, uses leading underscores to indicate that a name is intended to be private. My understanding is that this library deviates from this convention by instead using leading underscores to limit what is included in the Sphinx-generated documentation. Names with leading underscores defined at module level are often used in other modules, and names with leading underscores defined at class level are often used outside the class, and even from other modules. In effect, such names are treated as private to the _package_. Is this understanding correct?

I would suggest looking into controlling visibility in the Sphinx-generated documentation separately and gradually migrating to normal Python usage.

issue_title: Spanner ignores provided credentials when executing a query.
issue_labels: [{'id': 539494117, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/api:%20spanner', 'name': 'api: spanner', 'color': 'cd7f32', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604244, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/priority:%20p1', 'name': 'priority: p1', 'color': 'ffa03e', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604235, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/status:%20acknowledged', 'name': 'status: acknowledged', 'color': 'fef2c0', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
(This bug is re-posted from an e-mail I received yesterday.)

----------------------

We are using python spanner client libraries, and I have a credentials issue in executing a sql query. Seems like executing a sql ignores the provided credentials to the client and just try to use the default credentials.

The code looks like:

```python
spanner_client = spanner.Client(credentials=credentials, project=project_id)
instance = spanner_client.instance(instance_id)
database = instance.database(database_id)
database.execute_sql(stmt)
```

Debugging the code for a bit, I found out that eventually the code [will try to create a session](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/spanner/google/cloud/spanner/session.py#L97) and it will [try to get the spanner API client](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/spanner/google/cloud//spanner/database.py#L157). When this object created, credentials is not passed and hence it's `None`, and hence the SpannerClient is trying to get the default credentials and fails in my case because I do not have default creds.

issue_title: The Python BigQuery client fails on creating a view in standardSQL
issue_body:
```python
from google.cloud import bigquery

bigquery_client = bigquery.Client(project="my-project")
dataset = bigquery_client.dataset("my-dataset")

table = dataset.table("v_test")

#Fails on Standard SQL
#table._properties['view'] = {'query': "select * from `my-project.my.dataset.test_table`",'useLegacySql':False}  

#Works on Legacy SQL
table._properties['view'] = {'query': "select * from [my-project:my-dataset.test_table]",'useLegacySql':True}  

try:
    table.create()
except Exception as err:
    print(err)
```
The https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/bigquery/google/cloud/bigquery/table.py#L355 should also take in another property for useLegacySql

Example  payload at http://stackoverflow.com/questions/33243427/how-can-i-create-a-new-view-in-bigquery-using-the-python-api

issue_title: bootstrap 4 Appended Text not working as expected for select fields
issue_body:
* Package version: 1.7.2
* Django version: 1.11.13
* Python version: 3.6.5
* Template pack: bootstrap4

### Description:

When appending a bootstrap.AppendedText to a layout for a select field, I expect to get something like the second example shown here:

https://getbootstrap.com/docs/4.0/components/input-group/#custom-select

This shows bootstrap 4 supports appending text to select inputs.

Instead the html generated by crispy-forms is missing both the div .input-group-append and the enclosing div .input-group.

The bug seems to be in `crispy_forms/templates/bootstrap4/layout/prepended_appended_text.html` which I intend to fix.

###  Example Crispy Forms code to reproduce

```
def __init__(self):
  self.helper = FormHelper()

  self.fields['select'] = forms.ChoiceField(label="Sample Label", help_text="Some help text", choices=[(1, "Choice 1"), (2, "Choice 2")])  # Choice fields are rendered with a select widget

  self.helper.layout = Layout(
    AppendedText('select', 'Some appended text')
  )
```

issue_title: How to override methods from extended UserAdmin
issue_body:
I am currently using the method described in the [Django 1.4 docs](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.4/topics/auth/#adding-userprofile-fields-to-the-admin) for extending the user admin with extra properties and methods in a Django 1.8 setup, and I am not able to make use of advanced features of django-import-export.

(This documentation is not available in newer versions. Is this not the preferred way to extend the user model, anymore?)

E.g. any attempt to override a method (e.g. `before_import()`) simply fails. Here is an excerpt of my code:

```
from django.contrib.auth.models import User
from django.contrib.auth.admin import UserAdmin

from import_export import resources
from import_export.admin import ImportExportMixin

class UserProfileResource(resources.ModelResource):
  def before_import(self, dataset, dry_run):
    print 123

  class Meta:
    model = UserProfile
    resource_class = resources.modelresource_factory(UserProfile)

class UserProfileAdmin(ImportExportMixin, UserAdmin):
  …

admin.site.unregister(User)
admin.site.register(User, UserProfileAdmin)
```

(Please let me know if you think details of `UserProfileAdmin` might be important to solve the issue.)

I’d expect to see `123` in the log output whenever I import user data but this is not the case.

Digging a little bit deeper in the django-import-export code I found that the actual resource class seems to be not the one I desire.

This is the output when I log its actual value and the corresponding model’s name from within the method `get_import_resource_class()` in [line 117 of admin.py](https://github.com/django-import-export/django-import-export/blob/master/import_export/admin.py#L117) 

```
def get_resource_class(self):
  print self.model
  print modelresource_factory(self.model)

<class 'django.contrib.auth.models.User'
<class 'import_export.resources.UserResource'>
```

IMHO it should be `UserProfile` and `UserProfileResource` instead.

Of course, I simply tried to rename my resource class to `UserResource` but it did not help.

Is there a work-around for this issue? I am aware that this could be unrelated to django-import-export and rather lie in the nature of Django mixins but I would be glad about any help, anyway.

Cheers,
tobi

issue_title: Configparser does not follow includes
issue_body:
There is a config like:

```ini
[core]
# ...
[include]
    path = ~/path/to/another/config
```

When using this code, all configs from the other config will be ignored:

```py
cfg = git.Repo().config_reader()
cfg.read()
```

issue_title: When diffing index 'HEAD', can't get 'A' change_type but 'D'
issue_body:
So, this issue is about
```
git diff HEAD
```

I tried several times doing repo.index.diff('HEAD') on modified or on added files. On modified files it worked like charm. But on new files, gitPython doesn't return diff with change_type=='A' but 'D'.

I tried on gitpython 2.0.8 with python3 on Linux, and 2.0.9.dev(pyPI ver) on Windows 7. Resulted same.

here's what i did

```terminal
milhankim@LGESELMil:~/sandbox/docker$ touch add_test && git add add_test && ipython3
Python 3.5.2+ (default, Sep 22 2016, 12:18:14) 

In [1]: import git

In [2]: repo = git.Repo('.')

In [3]: repo.index.diff('HEAD')
Out[3]: [<git.diff.Diff at 0x7ffbd021a268>]

In [4]: repo.index.diff('HEAD')[0].change_type
Out[4]: 'D'

In [5]: repo.index.diff('HEAD')[0].new_file
Out[5]: False

In [6]: repo.index.diff('HEAD')[0].deleted_file
Out[6]: True

milhankim@LGESELMil:~/sandbox/docker$ git diff HEAD
diff --git a/add_test b/add_test
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..e69de29
```

issue_title: tool for creating partial nwb files
issue_body:
## Feature Request

### Problem/Use Case
I have heard from several groups that they have the desire to store stimulus data in an external file and link to the file. This is important for them because they often play the exact same stimulus and do not want to duplicate the data for every NWB file. Sometimes the stimulus file is big (e.g. videos) and even when it is not that big it's a bad idea to duplicate data. This is a critical issue for many labs. (@the-moliver)

Another application for externally linking files is for subject data. For instance, brain imaging data that is constant for a subject could be stored externally and linked to. I have this issue with the Chang Lab, where I would really like to not store the cortical surface mesh of each subject every time I record an experimental block (there are often >100 per subject).

We support this! (#408) However it's not easy to create the file that is to be linked to and there isn't really a convention for doing this. I would like a tool that writes a file containing a single dataset or a small set of datasets. This file is not strictly nwb-compliant because it does not contain all the necessary parts (processing, acquisition, etc.) but the dataset(s) created are compliant and fit perfectly in to an nwb file when linked to. I propose that these files be designated by .nwbaux and we should have a tool to create them:

```python
with NWBHDF5IO('subject_001.nwbaux', 'mode='w') as io:
    io.write_aux_file(container_or_list_of_containers)
```

I'd like to open the discussion here. Is this a good idea? Do others think this would be useful? Any suggestions for a better naming convention (I'd prefer .nwb-aux but is a dash in the file suffix a bad idea?)
